serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of watermelon is 2–3 slices (approximately 200g), commonly consumed as a snack or dessert fruit in Singapore and across Southeast Asia.

nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, calcium, iron, saturated fat, cholesterol, trans fat, vitamin C) sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 167765 (Watermelon, raw), an approved primary source. HPB Singapore Foodo database values for energy (30 kcal), protein (0.61g), fat (0.15g), carbohydrate (7.55g), sugar (6.2g), sodium (1mg), dietary fibre (0.4g), calcium (7mg), and iron (0.24mg) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central and have been retained. USDA FoodData Central is used as the primary cited source to ensure full approved-source compliance across all nutrient fields including cholesterol (0mg), saturated fat (0.02g), trans fat (0g), and vitamin C (8.1mg/100g). The grabfood source present in the input record has been replaced entirely.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 76 for watermelon (raw) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283 — a peer-reviewed journal publication. Both are approved GI sources per Project Veritas v2 source policy. The input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source; this has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed citation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (76 × 15.1g) / 100 = 11.5; available carbohydrate per 200g serving = 7.55g × 2 = 15.1g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1mg/100g); contains potassium (112mg/100g) and citrulline supporting vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Watermelon is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content and potassium supply; consume in moderate portions (200g) as large volumes may trigger an insulin response that indirectly elevates blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 76) with low glycemic load per 100g (GL ~5.7); rapid glucose absorption risk at larger portions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit watermelon to small portions (100–150g) and pair with a protein or fat source to blunt the postprandial glucose spike; avoid watermelon juice as removal of fibre accelerates glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, very low saturated fat (0.02g/100g), contains lycopene and citrulline with cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Watermelon is appropriate for individuals managing cardiovascular disease; its lycopene content (approximately 4–6mg/100g) and citrulline support endothelial function and antioxidant defence, while its negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol pose no atherogenic risk at normal serving sizes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains lycopene, vitamin C (8.1mg/100g), and citrulline with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Watermelon may benefit individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its lycopene and vitamin C content; consume in moderate portions to avoid excess sugar load that may promote systemic inflammation at high intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Watermelon (Citrullus lanatus) is a large, round to oval fruit with a thick green-striped rind and sweet, juicy red flesh containing edible black seeds. It is consumed raw as a fresh fruit, snack, or dessert across Singapore, Southeast Asia, and globally. Nutritionally, watermelon is very low in calories (30 kcal/100g) and fat (0.15g/100g), with moderate natural sugars (6.2g/100g) and minimal dietary fibre (0.4g/100g). It is an excellent source of hydration, with approximately 91% water content. Watermelon contains lycopene (a potent carotenoid antioxidant), vitamin C (8.1mg/100g), potassium (112mg/100g), and the amino acid citrulline, which supports nitric oxide production and vasodilation. Despite its low glycemic load at small portions, watermelon has a high glycemic index (GI 76), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is naturally free of cholesterol, gluten, and dairy, and is suitable for vegan, vegetarian, and halal diets. In Singapore, watermelon is commonly sold sliced at hawker centres, supermarkets, and fruit stalls, and is a popular snack during hot weather. Known as 西瓜 (xī guā) in Chinese, Tembikai in Malay, தர்பூசணி in Tamil, and तरबूज in Hindi. Source: USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 167765; GI per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of watermelon?
Is watermelon good for high blood pressure?
Can I eat watermelon on a diet?
Is watermelon good for heart health?
How much watermelon can a diabetic eat?
